summary: The posterior triangle is bounded by the sternocleidomastoid anteriorly, trapezius posteriorly, and middle third of the clavicle inferiorly.
The posterior triangle is bounded by the trapezius anteriorly and sternocleidomastoid posteriorly.
The anterior border of the posterior triangle is the omohyoid muscle.
Erb's palsy (C5-C6 upper trunk injury) causes the waiter's tip position: adducted shoulder, extended elbow, pronated forearm, and flexed wrist.
Erb's palsy affects C8-T1 and causes claw hand.
Erb's palsy causes wrist drop and finger drop.
The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) crosses the posterior triangle superficially and is vulnerable during lymph node biopsy; its damage causes trapezius paralysis.
The spinal accessory nerve runs deep to the prevertebral fascia and is well protected.
Spinal accessory nerve damage causes paralysis of the sternocleidomastoid only.
In torticollis, fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid causes the head to tilt toward the affected side with the chin rotated to the opposite side.
In torticollis, the chin rotates toward the same side as the affected muscle.
Torticollis is caused by fibrosis of the trapezius muscle.
The brachial plexus roots emerge between the scalenus anterior and scalenus medius muscles in the interscalene triangle.
The brachial plexus roots emerge posterior to scalenus medius.
The brachial plexus roots pass through the scalenus anterior muscle.
Klumpke's palsy (C8-T1 lower trunk injury) causes claw hand deformity with weakness of intrinsic hand muscles and may have Horner's syndrome if T1 is involved.
Klumpke's palsy causes the waiter's tip position.
Klumpke's palsy affects the upper trunk of the brachial plexus.
